title: Australian F2: Grunt Triumphs Through Chaos to Launch 2026 Campaign
caption: Jaromir Grunt celebrates an unexpected victory at Albert Park.
body:
Melbourne’s Albert Park hosted the 2026 F2 season opener and delivered exactly the kind of race the category specialises in — relentless, unpredictable, and with a result no pre-race projection would have confidently called at its outset. Jaromir Grunt emerged victorious for AIX Racing, but the route to that result wound through a red flag, a safety car, a devastating teammate collision, a sudden puncture, and a ferocious multi-car battle on the final lap. The Czech driver, entirely anonymous in practice, seized pole in a qualifying thriller and never let go, absorbing every shock the afternoon could throw at him.

Practice: A Weekend of Mixed Form

Three free practice sessions produced three different pacesetters, hinting at the unpredictability to come. Takumi Aizawa (Campos Racing) set the opening benchmark in FP1 with a 1:22.576, narrowly edging Andrew Price (Prema) and Yann Asdelian (DAMS). FP2 saw Colin Hongnoi (Rodin Motorsport) top the times with a 1:22.928, while Hamish Porter and Tim Mittens showed strong one-lap speed. In FP3, Daniel Park (Trident) laid down an eye-catching 1:18.117, with Mittens again second and Jacob Vavro third. Notably, Jaromir Grunt was nowhere near the top of any practice sheet, failing to crack the top ten in any session. Few would have predicted what came next.

Qualifying: Grunt Steals Pole by Six Thousandths

Qualifying unravelled across three tense phases. Q1 saw Park go fastest as a late surge from Tim Mittens and Kanata Rivington eliminated early favourite Andrew Price, who had topped FP1 but could not reproduce that speed when it mattered. Ryan Melon, Marek Dubišar, and Jacob Vavro also fell at the first hurdle. Q2 tightened the screw further: Aizawa and Rivington traded fastest laps before Colin Hongnoi and Marcos Olmedo delivered knockout blows in the closing seconds, sending Mittens, Brock Lee, Daniel Park, and Alex Thompson out of the session.

Q3 came down to the final thousandths. Korv Batt briefly held provisional pole, only for Jaromir Grunt — who had been nowhere all weekend — to produce a sensational three-purple-sector lap of 1:21.888. Kodiak Callahan (DAMS) came within 0.006 seconds of stealing it, a margin that haunted the Australian on home soil. Marcos Olmedo and Colin Hongnoi filled row two, while Batt was left ruing a messy middle sector that cost him a shot at the front. It was a pole position that stunned the paddock and set the stage for a chaotic Sunday.

Race: Premeur’s Heavy Crash Halts the Action

The feature race was barely minutes old when the red flag flew. Corentin Premeur, running for ART Grand Prix, was involved in a heavy crash at Turn 5, his car left stricken and the barriers requiring repair. The incident removed one of the pre-race wildcards from contention and forced a full restart, compressing the field and resetting tyre strategies before any meaningful pit window had opened. Premeur emerged unhurt but his weekend was over.

At the restart, Grunt reasserted control, holding off Batt into Turn 1 and quickly building a 2.4-second cushion. Kodiak Callahan slotted into third, while Hamish Porter (Invicta Racing) and Tim Mittens (Hitech Pulse-Eight) began to apply pressure. Mittens, the picture of consistency all weekend, pulled off two back-to-back overtakes to climb into the top six, his Hitech looking racy on the medium compound. Behind them, the midfield was a restless swarm, drivers jostling for position while trying to keep an eye on the opening pit-stop window.

Strategic Chess: Undercuts, Lead Changes, and a Puncture

The pit window fractured the order completely. Porter and Callahan were among the first to blink, triggering an undercut battle that reshaped the podium fight. Porter’s stop was swift and decisive, vaulting him ahead of Callahan and into a net second place. Grunt stayed out longer, stretching his lead before pitting and emerging just ahead of Batt — who then suffered a sticky wheel gun issue that cost precious seconds and dropped him down the order. As the field cycled through, late-stoppers Jacob Vavro and Colin Hongnoi briefly inherited the lead, the pair gambling on track position. But the gamble unravelled: Hongnoi suffered a sudden puncture shortly after his stop, a devastating blow that destroyed his afternoon, while Vavro’s ambitious strategy ended in retirement when he crashed out in the final third of the race — though not before setting the outright fastest lap of the race, a searing 1:19.182, a bittersweet footnote for AIX Racing.

Safety Car Carnage: Campos Teammates Collide

The race’s decisive chapter arrived with a sickening thud. Under pressure in the midfield, Campos Racing teammates Takumi Aizawa and Marcos Olmedo made contact, the two cars tangling and spearing into the barriers. Both were eliminated instantly, the collision ending a weekend that had promised so much for the Spanish team. Aizawa, fastest in FP1, had been a genuine threat; Olmedo had qualified fourth. Instead, the team left Melbourne with a double DNF and a paddock full of questions. The safety car was deployed, erasing Grunt’s commanding 9.5-second lead and bunching the field for a sprint to the finish.

At the restart, Grunt led Porter, with Callahan in third — but Tim Mittens had other ideas. The American lunged past Callahan at Turn 3 to snatch the final podium position, the Haas Academy driver refusing to let go. Korv Batt, charging back from his pit-stop delay, then sliced past Callahan for fourth, and Yann Asdelian (DAMS) pulled off the uncomfortable move of passing his own teammate Callahan for fifth. Callahan’s race had unravelled from 0.006 seconds off pole to a lonely seventh, his home hopes dashed in a handful of corners.

Final Lap Shootout: Mittens Holds Off the Pack

The final lap was a classic F2 knife-fight. Mittens found himself under relentless pressure from Batt and Asdelian, the three cars running almost nose-to-tail through Albert Park’s fast sweepers. Batt looked to the inside at Turn 9, Asdelian probed around the outside at Turn 11, but Mittens — ice-cool under braking — placed his car perfectly each time, crossing the line just ahead of Batt to secure his first podium of the season. Asdelian finished a valiant fifth, while Daniel Park (Trident) collected solid points in sixth, a strong start for the Mercedes Academy driver.

Grunt’s Masterclass: From Anonymous to Victorious

Jaromir Grunt crossed the line 8.877 seconds clear of Porter, a margin that flattered the chaos behind him. The AIX Racing driver had led every lap of the restarted race, managing the red flag, the safety car, and two restarts with a composure that belied his rookie status. It was a lights-to-flag victory that absorbed every shock without ever looking threatened. Combined with pole position, Grunt leaves Australia with a perfect 25-point haul and the championship lead — a position that seemed unthinkable when practice ended on Saturday.

Winners and Losers

Winners: Jaromir Grunt — from zero practice pace to pole and a dominant win. Hamish Porter — a smart, composed drive and a well-executed undercut gave Invicta Racing a strong second. Tim Mittens — top-three in every practice session, out in Q2, and a last-lap podium rewarded relentless pace. Frederik Frid (Van Amersfoort Racing) and Daniel Park — quiet but effective drives into the points, promising building blocks for the season.

Losers: Campos Racing — a double DNF after a teammate collision, an unmitigated disaster. Kodiak Callahan — 0.006s from pole, ended seventh and was passed by his own teammate. Andrew Price — fastest in FP1, out in Q1. Colin Hongnoi — a puncture robbed him of a likely points finish. Corentin Premeur — the red flag was a brutal end to a weekend that never got started.

What They Said

Jaromir Grunt (1st): "I wasn’t on anyone’s radar after practice — honestly, neither was I. But the car just came alive in qualifying, and today I just had to hit my marks and not get caught up in the madness. The restarts were tense, but the team kept me calm. To win from pole in my first race of the season is a dream."

Hamish Porter (2nd): "We played the strategy perfectly. The undercut was the key — I knew if I could jump Callahan, I could control the gap. I’m a little disappointed we couldn’t challenge Jaromir for the win, but 18 points is a fantastic way to start the year."

Tim Mittens (3rd): "Consistency all weekend paid off. The last lap was insane, three-wide into Turn 3 at one point. I just focused on hitting my apexes and not giving them a sniff. This podium tastes sweet after getting knocked out in Q2."

Takumi Aizawa (DNF, Campos): "A nightmare ending. We had the pace all weekend — I was quickest in practice — but one moment of misjudgment with my teammate and it’s over. I’m sorry to the team. We have to make sure this never happens again."

Kodiak Callahan (7th): "0.006 seconds from pole and then to finish seventh is a tough pill. The restart didn’t help, and I just didn’t have the pace on the mediums. We’ll bounce back in Bahrain."

Championship Outlook

Grunt leads the drivers’ standings with 25 points, followed by Porter on 18 and Mittens on 15. The field’s density at the top — Batt (12), Asdelian (10), and Park (8) are all within striking distance — suggests this lead will be fiercely contested. Invicta Racing’s Porter took a commanding second, while Van Amersfoort Racing’s Frid also scored well in eighth, giving both teams early-season points and confidence. Campos will be desperate to reset after a scoreless and bruising opener. The paddock now packs up and heads to Bahrain, where the desert heat and a very different circuit will ask new questions. The 2026 F2 season, it is already clear, will be a brawl from start to finish.
